item.title: PixelSmash FFmpeg RCE (CVE-2026-8461), FortiBleed Credential Sniffer, ShapedPlugin Backdoor, and AI Agent Compromise Vectors
item.content:encoded:
FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials — Must Read

The FortiBleed campaign uses a custom sniffer tool (FortigateSniffer) deployed on compromised FortiGate firewalls to capture authentication credentials from network traffic, which attackers then crack with a rented GPU cluster to gain network access.

  • SOCRadar discovered that the FortiBleed campaign uses a custom Go-based sniffer called FortigateSniffer that abuses the FortiOS diagnose sniffer packet feature to capture authentication credentials.
  • Attackers gained admin access to FortiGate devices through credential stuffing and brute-force attacks before deploying the sniffer to harvest secrets from traffic across 24 protocols.
  • The stolen packets were converted to PCAP files via a component named SNIFTRAN and analyzed with a Python toolkit to extract cleartext credentials and hashes, which were cracked using Hashcat on a rented cluster of 36 enterprise GPUs.
  • The campaign has been active since at least February 2026 and has targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls worldwide, with the threat actor acting as an initial access broker.
  • Kevin Beaumont published a list of IP addresses associated with the campaign to help organizations determine if they were targeted.

ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack — Must Read

Legitimate updates for ShapedPlugin's Pro WordPress plugins were backdoored in a supply chain attack, injecting credential-stealing malware that captures plaintext passwords, 2FA codes, and establishes persistent remote access.

  • Unknown threat actors compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro plugin releases distributed through official licensed update channels.
  • The backdoor malware installs a fake plugin that hides itself, steals plaintext credentials and two-factor authentication codes, and establishes persistent remote access via a web shell and a custom REST endpoint.
  • Only Pro versions of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro distributed through the vendor's Easy Digital Downloads infrastructure are affected; free versions on WordPress.org are not impacted.
  • The incident is tracked under CVE-2026-49777 (CVSS 10.0) for the Product Slider Pro compromise and CVE-2026-10735 (CVSS 9.8) for the overall supply chain attack.
  • ShapedPlugin confirmed the compromise and is reviewing its distribution and release processes, with new versions pending comprehensive security reviews.

Operation Endgame Targets SocGholish: What It Means for Defenders — Must Read

International law enforcement operation Endgame disrupted the SocGholish malware delivery infrastructure operated by TA569, highlighting how targeting initial access broker operations can cascade disruption across multiple downstream criminal groups.

  • Operation Endgame is a coordinated international law enforcement action that disrupted SocGholish infrastructure, including the remediation of thousands of compromised websites.
  • SocGholish is a malware delivery framework operated by TA569 that compromises legitimate websites and uses fake browser update prompts to deliver malware.
  • TA569 operated as an initial access broker, selling access to victim environments to downstream criminal groups such as ransomware operators.
  • Disrupting an initial access provider can have cascading effects across multiple criminal ecosystems that rely on that access.
  • Silent Push previously published research detailing TA569's infrastructure, traffic distribution systems, victim filtering mechanisms, and criminal partnerships.

AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network — Must Read

AryStinger malware has hijacked over 4,300 outdated routers to build a stealthy reconnaissance proxy network, enabling attackers to scan and probe targets while hiding their origin.

  • AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers, mostly D-Link DIR-850L models, by exploiting CVE-2013-3307 and CVE-2016-5681.
  • The malware turns infected routers into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network, scanning the internet, tunneling traffic, and running commands to hide the attacker's location.
  • A second variant targets QNAP NAS devices through CVE-2025-11837, a code injection flaw in QNAP's Malware Remover tool, though NAS infection counts are not yet known.
  • Infected devices are concentrated in South Korea (about 48%) and China (about 32%), with additional infections in Sweden, Malaysia, and Singapore.
  • QiAnXin XLab first detected the campaign on March 12, 2026, originating from IP address 107.150.106.14, and the number of infected routers continues to rise.

New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer — Must Read

A new, evasive malware loader named OXLOADER is being distributed via malicious Google Ads to deliver the CastleStealer infostealer, targeting users outside the CIS region with sophisticated anti-analysis and obfuscation techniques.

  • Elastic Security Labs discovered a new campaign, REF8372, that deploys a previously unreported loader called OXLOADER to deliver the CastleStealer infostealer.
  • The attack starts with malicious Google Ads impersonating a Node.js download, redirecting users to a fake website that serves a batch script hosted on the Storj cloud storage platform.
  • OXLOADER uses multiple obfuscation layers including control-flow flattening, mixed Boolean-Arithmetic, and self-modifying decryption stubs, and abuses the Windows .reloc section to stage shellcode.
  • The threat actor is likely Russian-speaking and financially motivated, as the malware explicitly excludes machines located in Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) countries.
  • CastleStealer, the final payload, is a .NET-based information stealer previously associated with the CastleLoader family and the GrayBravo activity cluster.

PixelSmash – Critical FFmpeg Vulnerability Turns Media Files into Weapons — Must Read

A critical heap out-of-bounds write vulnerability (CVE-2026-8461, PixelSmash) in FFmpeg's MagicYUV decoder enables remote code execution via a crafted video file, impacting virtually any media-processing application that links FFmpeg, including Jellyfin, Nextcloud, and desktop players.

  • A heap out-of-bounds write vulnerability (CVE-2026-8461) in FFmpeg's MagicYUV decoder is triggered by a crafted video file containing malformed slice height values.
  • JFrog researchers escalated the flaw from a crash to reliable remote code execution on Jellyfin and Nextcloud servers by overwriting an AVBuffer function pointer to call system().
  • Because FFmpeg's libavcodec is a default dependency in countless media-handling applications, the vulnerability silently affects desktop players, media servers, file-manager thumbnail generators, and AI/ML inference pipelines.
  • The vulnerability was reported to the FFmpeg security team on May 13, 2026, and a patched version (8.1.2) was released on June 17, 2026; rebuilding FFmpeg without the MagicYUV decoder or applying a minimal source patch provides immediate mitigation.
  • PixelSmash exemplifies a supply-chain vulnerability: a single bug in a default-enabled decoder cascades to hundreds of downstream projects that inherit FFmpeg without any explicit opt-in or visibility into the codec-specific code.

How a Poisoned Coding Test Turned an AI Agent Into an Attacker — Should Read

AI coding agents introduce a new trust boundary where adversaries can plant hidden instructions in repositories to autonomously harvest credentials and exfiltrate data without dropping malware, and the lasting damage—stolen long-lived cloud credentials—persists beyond endpoint cleanup.

  • The attack used no malware, only hidden instructions in files an AI coding agent trusts by default, such as CLAUDE.md, .cursor/rules, README, and MCP configuration.
  • With auto-run enabled, the AI agent harvested AWS credentials, enumerated cloud and Kubernetes environments, discovered additional secrets, and exfiltrated the data via a poisoned Model Context Protocol server in under two minutes.
  • The lasting damage was the theft of a long-lived CI/CD cloud credential, which gave the attacker persistent access to production cloud infrastructure that survived workstation cleanup.
  • Endpoint controls and prompt guardrails did not stop the attack; effective defenses include short-lived credentials, isolated repositories, and runtime detection of anomalous identity behavior.
  • AI agents create a new trust boundary because adversaries can compromise systems by embedding malicious instructions in content the agent consumes, rather than attacking the developer directly.

Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil — Should Read

A suspected cyberattack on Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system sent unauthorized emergency alerts with the word 'misanthropy' to phones across multiple states, prompting authorities to suspend the platform and launch a federal investigation.

  • At least ten unauthorized emergency alerts containing the word 'misanthropy' were sent through Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system, overriding silent mode and sounding loud alarms on mobile phones across several states.
  • The false alerts affected residents in São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Paraná, Mato Grosso do Sul, and the Federal District.
  • Authorities suspended the alert platform, blocked external access to the Public Alert Dissemination Interface, and the Federal Police is investigating the unauthorized remote access by an individual outside the national civil defense network.
  • There is no evidence of structural damage to the core infrastructure, and a more secure version of the platform is under development, though no timeline for restoration has been given.

A VBScript campaign distributed through WhatsApp deploying RMM software — Should Read

A campaign uses compromised WhatsApp accounts to distribute VBScript files disguised as financial documents, which silently install legitimate remote management software to gain persistent access to victims' systems.

  • The campaign uses compromised WhatsApp accounts to send VBScript attachments disguised as financial documents, primarily targeting WhatsApp Desktop and Web users.
  • The multi-stage infection chain culminates in the silent installation of a ManageEngine Endpoint Central agent, which grants attackers remote control of the victim’s device.
  • Over 80% of detected victims were located in Malaysia, with infections also seen in Brazil, India, Mexico, Russia, and several other countries.
  • Limited evidence, such as Chinese-language comments in the scripts and infrastructure overlaps with ValleyRAT and Gh0st RAT, points to a possible Chinese-speaking operator, but definitive attribution is not established.

Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants — Should Read

Zafran Security found DifyTap, a set of critical flaws in the Dify AI platform that could let attackers silently read private AI conversations across different tenants, with some vulnerabilities requiring no authentication.

  • Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities in the Dify AI platform, collectively called DifyTap, enabling attackers to read private AI chats across different tenants.
  • Two of the vulnerabilities required no authentication, and three allowed cross-tenant data exposure, putting one customer's data at risk of being read by another.
  • The flaws include CVE-2026-41947 through CVE-2026-41950, with authorization bypasses and a path traversal permitting unauthorized access to trace configurations, internal plugin APIs, and uploaded files.
  • Attackers can set up a persistent exfiltration channel by redirecting all messages and responses from any accessible application to an attacker-controlled trace provider.
  • All vulnerabilities except CVE-2026-41948 were fixed in Dify version 1.14.2, with a patch for the remaining flaw planned for the next release.

MCP Token Theft in Claude Code: A Man-in-the-Middle Attack Chain — Should Read

A malicious npm package with a postinstall hook can silently rewrite Claude Code's MCP configuration to route OAuth tokens through an attacker-controlled proxy, enabling persistent token theft that survives rotation because the hook re-establishes the redirect. The primary lesson is that even valid, user-authorized MCP integrations can be hijacked without detection through simple file edits.

  • Claude Code's configuration file `~/.claude.json` serves as the control point for MCP server URLs and trust state, making it a pivotal file for the attack chain.
  • A user-level postinstall hook from a malicious npm package can rewrite MCP server endpoints to point to attacker-controlled infrastructure, enabling man-in-the-middle interception of OAuth tokens.
  • Provider-side audit logs show valid OAuth traffic from Anthropic's egress IP range, so the token theft remains invisible to standard security monitoring.
  • Token rotation fails to remediate the compromise because the hook automatically reasserts the malicious configuration each time Claude Code loads, giving the attacker persistent access.
  • Detection should focus on changes to Claude Code configuration, unexpected MCP server URLs, localhost proxies, OAuth refresh patterns, and SaaS actions that do not match the user's normal work.

AI Agent Supply Chain Risk: Silent Codebase Exfiltration via Skills — Should Read

AI agent skills can be weaponized to silently exfiltrate entire codebases through hidden instructions and a crafted Definition of Done, turning a one-click skill install into a full supply chain leak with no audit trail.

  • Mitiga Labs created a malicious AI agent skill disguised as a test generator that silently exfiltrated an entire local codebase to an attacker's remote public repository.
  • The attack used a crafted Definition of Done clause to force the AI agent to verify the exfiltration was complete, while leaving the agent's audit log empty.
  • The malicious skill required only four user interactions, showing how a seemingly legitimate skill can lead to full codebase leakage with minimal user engagement.
  • Adversaries could inflate a skill's reputation on public catalogs like skills.sh using bots and fake downloads to make the skill appear trusted and widely used.
  • The article advises organizations to review skill instructions for silent operators and to evaluate AI agent behaviors before approving skill installations.

Claude Code + Slack: How an AI Agent Integration Becomes a Compromise Path — Should Read

Malicious AI agent skills can turn a compromised Claude Code integration into a trusted phishing distribution channel across Slack, inheriting the victim's identity and reaching the entire organization in minutes.

  • Malicious skills turn Claude Code compromises into a phishing distribution channel across Slack, inheriting the trust of the user's account.
  • Claude Code payloads spread organization-wide in minutes, reaching multiple channels and DMs from a familiar sender.
  • Attackers embed instructions in skill guidelines and fallback behavior to push agents toward executing malicious actions without hesitation.
  • Treat skills like software dependencies: review them, version them, log invocations, and scope permissions narrowly.

ConsentFix: OAuth Phishing That Bypasses MFA in Microsoft Entra ID — Should Read

ConsentFix chains OAuth consent phishing with ClickFix-style social engineering to steal Microsoft Entra ID tokens without credentials or MFA, showing that defenses must extend to token protection, monitoring, and restricting trusted first-party app access.

  • ConsentFix is a browser-based phishing attack that tricks users into providing an OAuth authorization code, which the attacker redeems for access and refresh tokens without requiring credentials or triggering multi-factor authentication.
  • The attack exploits first-party Microsoft applications like Azure CLI that are pre-trusted in Entra ID tenants, allowing attackers to bypass consent prompts and inherit the victim's delegated permissions.
  • Organizations can defend against ConsentFix by enabling token protection in Conditional Access policies, restricting access to first-party apps, educating users never to share localhost URLs, and continuously monitoring for anomalous token usage.
  • Because the initial sign-in is legitimate, ConsentFix evades many traditional security controls, making detection dependent on visibility into post-authentication activity across cloud and identity environments.
  • A successful ConsentFix attack can lead to full account takeover, data exfiltration, and lateral movement across Microsoft 365, Azure, and connected SaaS applications, with risks amplified for high-privilege users.

Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries — Should Read

Google will enforce mandatory developer identity verification for Android app installs in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand starting September 30, 2026, blocking normal installation of apps from unregistered developers and threatening independent open-source repositories like F-Droid.

  • Google will begin enforcing mandatory Android developer verification on September 30, 2026, in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand.
  • After the deadline, certified Android devices in these four countries will block the normal installation of apps from developers who have not registered their identity with Google.
  • The registration process requires developers to submit a legal name, address, contact details, possibly a government ID, and prove app ownership by providing an APK signed with their private key.
  • F-Droid, the free-software app repository, states that the verification requirement would end its project because its pseudonymous contributors will not hand legal identities to Google.
  • A Keep Android Open campaign, supported by more than 70 organizations in 23 countries, has asked Google to drop identity checks for apps distributed outside Google Play.

A Fake Scotiabank Voicemail Was Actually an HTML File Asking You to Call an Attacker — Should Read

A phishing email impersonating Scotiabank delivered an HTML file disguised as a voicemail attachment, with the entire attack hinging on a fake callback number to trick a CFO into calling the attacker directly—bypassing link-based security tools.

  • The attacker impersonated Scotiabank in an email to a CFO, falsely claiming an unauthorized $3,000 Interac e-Transfer and instructing the recipient to call a provided toll-free number to stop it.
  • The email was sent from a compromised small-business domain registered in 2015 that passed SPF and DMARC authentication, allowing it to bypass email reputation filters and land with the lowest spam score.
  • The attachment was an HTML file disguised with an MP3-like filename, which opened a page mimicking a Scotiabank notification and used Microsoft Dynamics Marketing infrastructure, but contained no malware or malicious link.
  • The entire attack was a callback phishing (TOAD) campaign that relied solely on the recipient dialing the attacker-controlled phone number, avoiding traditional link-based detection and leveraging voice-channel social engineering.
  • IRONSCALES flagged the message at 60% confidence based on behavioral patterns like VIP targeting, financial urgency, and the phone number as the primary call-to-action, despite the attachment and links appearing clean.

Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices — Should Read

Canada's intelligence agency CSIS obtained a first-of-its-kind warrant to remotely clean state-linked botnets from infected Canadian routers and IoT devices, mirroring earlier FBI operations against Chinese and Russian espionage networks.

  • Canada's CSIS used a threat reduction warrant for the first time to remotely delete botnet malware from privately owned servers, routers, and IoT devices located in Canada.
  • The warrant was necessary because the cleanup would otherwise have been a criminal act (computer mischief), and the court found the threat to Canada clearly established and imminent.
  • Two foreign state-run botnets were routing espionage traffic through unmaintained Canadian devices to probe critical infrastructure, including the energy sector.
  • The operation mirrored prior U.S. court-ordered botnet cleanups by the FBI but was conducted by an intelligence agency under different legal authority, and device owners may not have been told.

INTERPOL Warns Phishing, Ransomware, and AI Scams Are Rising Across Asia-Pacific — Should Read

INTERPOL warns of surging phishing, ransomware, and AI-powered scams across Asia-Pacific, with organized crime networks in Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, and the Philippines using deepfakes and forced labor to drive billions in cybercrime losses.

  • Phishing is the most widespread and financially damaging form of cybercrime in the Asia-Pacific region, with one-third of countries reporting more than 10,000 cases between January 2024 and March 2025.
  • More than 135,000 ransomware-related attacks were recorded in 2024, heavily impacting the real estate, manufacturing, and financial services sectors.
  • Organized crime groups in Cambodia, Laos, Myanmar, and the Philippines used deepfake technology in 'romance baiting' scams, blending AI personas and social engineering to fuel an estimated $37 billion in regional cybercrime losses.
  • Banking trojans and information stealers—specifically RedLine, Lumma, LokiBot, Negasteal, and ZBot—are the second most prevalent cyber threat in the region.
  • Distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks surged by 92% in 2024 compared to the prior year, and system intrusions accounted for approximately 80% of all data breaches.

Bypassing Microsoft Entra Conditional Access Policies via Nested App Authentication — Should Read

NetSPI discovered that abusing Nested App Authentication flows with specific Microsoft clients, such as ADIbizaUX, could bypass Entra Conditional Access Policies entirely to return Microsoft Graph API tokens; exploitation required a compromised Azure Portal refresh token and was subsequently patched by Microsoft.

  • NetSPI researchers found that Nested App Authentication (NAA) flows for certain Microsoft clients, such as ADIbizaUX, could return Microsoft Graph API tokens without being subject to Conditional Access Policies.
  • Exploiting this bypass required an attacker to first obtain a refresh token for the Azure Portal, achievable only through phishing or full credential compromise including MFA material.
  • The Azure Portal refresh token has a fixed 24-hour lifetime and cannot be extended, limiting the attack window.
  • Two additional vulnerable clients were identified: Microsoft Intune for Education portal extension and Microsoft Intune portal extension.
  • Microsoft subsequently patched the vulnerability, and the Conditional Access bypass is no longer possible.

Okta Logs Threat-Hunting Guide: Hunt for Breach Evidence — May Read

This guide details how to hunt for Okta breach evidence by monitoring critical log fields and events, and highlights logging gaps like unlogged API token usage and read-only admin console access that could obscure malicious activity.

  • The article advises focusing on specific Okta System Log events to detect malicious activity linked to the recent Okta breach by the Lapsus$ group.
  • API token usage cannot be distinguished in logs from actions of the user who created the token, and read-only access to the Okta Admin console is not logged at all, creating blind spots for unauthorized activity.
  • Legitimate single sign-on logins to services like Google Workspace can cause authentication events to appear from distant IP addresses belonging to those services, complicating location-based anomaly detection.
  • The combination of Okta and Jamf sends keep-alive events that reflect IP address changes, which incident responders can use to spot suspicious simultaneous sessions from different locations.

JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft — May Read

The JaredFromSubway MEV bot lost $15 million after an attacker deceived its opportunity-detection logic with fake pools and tokens, tricking it into approving attacker-controlled contracts that then drained WETH, USDC, and USDT.

  • The JaredFromSubway MEV bot lost $15 million after an attacker used fake pools and tokens to manipulate its opportunity-detection logic into approving attacker-controlled contracts.
  • The attacker deployed contracts that appeared as profitable MEV opportunities, causing the bot to grant ERC-20 token approvals that were later exploited via the transferFrom function to drain WETH, USDC, and USDT.
  • Over time, the attacker accumulated 92.1614 WETH in approved allowances without immediately using them, then withdrew the tokens in a coordinated final step.
  • JaredFromSubway is a private MEV bot known for aggressive sandwich attacks that profit from price movements caused by other traders' transactions, often to their disadvantage.
  • After the theft, JaredFromSubway offered a $7.5 million bounty for the return of 50% of the stolen funds and entered negotiations with an undisclosed white-hat hacking group.

29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests — May Read

A 29-year-old heap over-read bug in Squid proxy's FTP parser (CVE-2026-47729) leaks cleartext HTTP requests and credentials to other users on the same proxy; disabling FTP eliminates the risk.

  • A heap over-read vulnerability in Squid's FTP directory-listing parser (CVE-2026-47729) can leak cleartext HTTP requests, including credentials, to another user of the same proxy.
  • The bug requires an attacker with existing proxy access and an FTP server they control on port 21, both enabled by default in Squid.
  • The flaw sits in a whitespace-skipping loop introduced in 1997 that fails to stop at a string's null terminator, reading freed memory buffers that Squid reuses without zeroing.
  • The fix adds a null-terminator check before the vulnerable strchr calls and is included in Squid 7.7, with some distributions backporting it.
  • Disabling FTP entirely is the recommended mitigation since most networks carry almost no FTP traffic and it eliminates the attack surface.

Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution — May Read

A vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in Microsoft's AutoGen Studio AI framework could allow arbitrary command execution by tricking an agent into visiting a malicious page, but it was fixed before any official package release, limiting impact to developers building from the main GitHub branch during a short window.

  • The AutoJack vulnerability chain in Microsoft’s AutoGen Studio could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary commands on a developer’s machine by tricking a browsing AI agent into visiting a malicious webpage.
  • The flaw was identified and fixed during development before any official PyPI release, so it only affected users who manually built AutoGen Studio from the main GitHub branch during a limited window.
  • The attack exploited three weaknesses: the MCP WebSocket’s trust of localhost connections, missing authentication on /api/mcp/* routes, and a parameter that passed attacker-supplied commands to the process launcher.
  • Microsoft advises running AutoGen Studio in an isolated, low-privilege environment and never exposing it to the internet or untrusted content.

Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process — May Read

Microsoft confirmed that Windows 11 version 26H2 will roll out later this year as a small enablement package upgrade for devices running 24H2 or 25H2, requiring only a restart, while older versions need a full 6.5 GB update.

  • Microsoft confirmed that Windows 11 version 26H2 is the next feature update and is already being tested with Windows Insiders in the Dev Channel.
  • Devices running Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 can upgrade to 26H2 using a 174 KB enablement package that only requires a restart.
  • Older systems, such as those on Windows 11 23H2, cannot use the enablement package and must perform a full upgrade that downloads 6.5 GB of data.
  • The lightweight upgrade is possible because versions 24H2, 25H2, and 26H2 share the same servicing branch and underlying code.
  • Microsoft has not announced any changes to hardware requirements or a specific release date, though H2 updates typically arrive in the fall.

Hunting Conditional Access Policy Bypass in the Wild: Leveraging Malicious Browser Extensions for Seamless Initial Access — May Read

A technique leveraging the Microsoft Intune Company Portal client ID (9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223) can bypass Entra Conditional Access Policies to obtain Microsoft Graph access tokens from non-compliant devices, which malicious browser extensions can automate for stealthy token theft.

  • The Conditional Access Policy bypass uses the Intune Company Portal client ID (9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223) in an OAuth2 authorization request to obtain a code, which is then exchanged for a Microsoft Graph access token from a non-compliant, unmanaged device.
  • A malicious browser extension can automate this attack by silently intercepting the OAuth2 flow, capturing the authorization code and access tokens, and exfiltrating them to an attacker-controlled server in a Browser-in-the-Middle (BiTM) scenario.
  • To detect this activity, analysts can hunt for interactive sign-in logs where device details show IsManaged: False, IsCompliant: False, IsTrusted: False, errorCode is 0, the client ID matches the Intune portal, and the resource is Microsoft Graph or Microsoft AD Graph.
  • Mitigations include requiring MFA for all users, auditing non-compliant device authentications, minimizing Microsoft Graph API permissions to least privilege, and continuously monitoring Graph activity logs for anomalies.
  • Microsoft later addressed the vulnerability by fully descoping the resource permission that had allowed unauthorized access to Microsoft Graph from the Intune Company Portal application.

A Glimpse into the “Search Your Target” Market for Stolen Credentials — Optional

A 'search your target' underground market has emerged where threat actors offer query-based credential extraction from billions of infostealer-collected records, enabling targeted account takeover without the need to process bulk dumps manually.

  • Analysis of 470 underground forum posts published between January 2025 and June 2026 reveals a service that searches threat actors' infostealer databases for credentials matching a specific target.
  • Sellers advertise databases with billions of lines and offer filtering by domain, URL, geography, or email, delivering results in formats such as URL:LOGIN:PASS.
  • Customer feedback shows a gap between advertised promises and reality, with many delivered credentials being invalid, duplicated, or lower in volume.
  • This service functions as a processing layer that converts bulk stolen credential collections into targeted attack material, enabling account takeover without manually sifting through massive dumps.

SpecterOps and OpenAI: Helping to Build a New Security Frontier with Daybreak — Optional

SpecterOps announced a partnership with OpenAI's Daybreak program to integrate frontier AI into BloodHound for attack path prioritization and environment analysis, aiming to help defenders triage millions of attack paths and uncover configuration debt.

  • OpenAI announced the Daybreak Cyber Partner Program to expand frontier AI cybersecurity capabilities to industry partners, including SpecterOps.
  • SpecterOps is integrating AI into BloodHound to enable prioritized remediation guidance based on attacker intent, not just attack path topology.
  • Many organizations suffer from configuration debt that prevents them from accurately understanding their own identity environments, a problem SpecterOps aims to address with automated analysis.
  • GhostWorks, SpecterOps's AI cyber innovation lab, is applying OpenAI's capabilities to automate reverse engineering of attacker tooling and improve endpoint coverage.

Investigator’s Guide to Okta Logs: System Log Fields for Incident Response — Optional

Okta system logs provide valuable session-tracking fields like externalSessionId and transaction.id for incident response, but analysts must treat the built-in risk and device-fingerprint fields with caution due to documented inaccuracies.

  • The debugContext.debugData.risk field can be inaccurate due to errors in anomalous device detection and location detection.
  • The debugContext.debugData.deviceFingerprint field often shows significant overlap across users and cannot solely identify a specific device.
  • Investigators should track sessions by correlating on authenticationContext.externalSessionId, transaction.id, debugContext.debugData.emailRequestId, and debugContext.debugData.authnRequestId.
  • Combining deviceFingerprint with other indicators is recommended to accurately track malicious activity.
  • The article provides pseudocode for filtering Okta logs to isolate events from known malicious session or request IDs.

GCP Service Agents & Workload Identities in Cloud Audit Logs: A Forensic Guide — Optional

This forensic guide explains how to identify and distinguish Service Agents, Workload Identity Federation, GKE Workload Identity, and Workforce Identity Federation in Google Cloud Audit Logs, enabling accurate attribution of actions during cloud incident investigations.

  • Service Agents are Google-managed service accounts automatically created when an associated Google API is enabled, and their actions are recorded in audit logs with delegation information in the serviceAccountDelegationInfo section.
  • Workload Identity Federation allows external workloads to obtain short-lived GCP access tokens without using service account keys, and the original external identity appears in the principalSubject field of the serviceAccountDelegationInfo.
  • GKE Workload Identity Federation binds Kubernetes Service Accounts to Google Service Accounts, and audit logs show the originating Kubernetes identity via a principalSubject containing the namespace and service account name prefixed with svc.id.goog.
  • Workforce Identity Federation enables human users to authenticate to GCP using an external identity provider, with their federated identity recorded in the authenticationInfo.principalSubject field for traceability.
  • Identifying these identity patterns in Cloud Audit Logs is critical for forensic investigations because it allows analysts to attribute actions to the correct source, whether a workload, CI pipeline, or specific namespace.

GitHub Security Logs: What They Reveal and What to Hunt For — Optional

GitHub must be treated as a security-critical system, not just a DevOps tool — security teams should export and monitor audit, token, and workflow logs to detect token abuse, unauthorized access changes, and malicious workflow modifications that attackers use for persistence and lateral movement.

  • GitHub is a security-critical system that combines intellectual property, automation control, and long-lived credentials, making it a target for attackers seeking persistence and access to production environments.
  • GitHub's telemetry includes audit, user security, actions workflow, and app/OAuth logs, but investigations are complicated by gaps like missing source IPs for some events and token ownership that is not always clear.
  • Effective threat hunting involves tracking MFA and credential changes, monitoring workflow file modifications, analyzing token usage patterns over time, and alerting on unauthorized GitHub App installations.
  • Repository deletions can erase access history if logs are not streamed, and internal-to-external forks are a subtle exfiltration path that often hides among high event volumes.

Defining Blast Radius in Cloud Incidents: Problem Scoping Guide | Mitiga — Optional

In cloud-native environments, the blast radius of an incident is defined by identity trust relationships and service permissions rather than network boundaries, making automated, log-driven scoping essential to quickly contain breaches.

  • The speed and accuracy of initial scoping determine whether a cloud incident is a minor disruption or a catastrophic breach.
  • Blast radius in cloud environments is shaped primarily by identity trust relationships and service permissions, not traditional network boundaries.
  • Because cloud resources are ephemeral, continuous forensic log retention is essential for scoping an attack timeline; without it, the ability to investigate disappears with the resources.
  • In Kubernetes, scoping requires visibility into container escapes, lateral pod-to-pod movement, and API server abuse, as a single compromised container can escalate to node-level and cluster-wide impact.
  • Ignoring identity-based lateral movement is a common scoping mistake, because a compromised role with assume-role permissions can cross account boundaries without touching network perimeters.

Introducing Patch the Planet — Optional

Trail of Bits launched Patch the Planet, using AI models like GPT-5.5-Cyber to discover and patch hundreds of vulnerabilities across critical open-source projects, shifting the security bottleneck from finding bugs to delivering maintainable fixes.

  • Trail of Bits launched the Patch the Planet initiative using GPT-5.5-Cyber to find and fix bugs across 19 critical open-source projects, producing 64 pull requests and 51 issues in the first week.
  • The effort delivered patches, fuzzing harnesses, CI security scanning, and long-term improvements, with 37 patches already merged, rather than simply filing bug reports.
  • The article states that AI has made bug discovery easy, shifting the expensive part of security to triage, confirmation, patching, and coordination with maintainers who are overwhelmed by AI-generated reports.
  • Maintainers are advised to use project-specific threat models and documentation like AGENTS.md to help AI tools filter false positives and correctly assess severity, reducing noise.
  • Technical achievements include building a fuzzing lab in under a day and using differential testing of cryptographic libraries to uncover real vulnerabilities such as an AES-GCM issue in PyCA.

Stop Your Legacy Infrastructure from Hijacking Your AI Agents — Optional

Attackers can hijack AI agents by chaining legacy infrastructure exposures—like unpatched servers and Active Directory misconfigurations—to reach the cloud resources agents depend on, without ever directly attacking the AI layer.

  • Attackers can compromise AI agents by chaining vulnerabilities in legacy infrastructure such as unpatched servers and Active Directory misconfigurations to reach the cloud resources those agents depend on.
  • A specific attack path demonstrated using CVE-2025-24813 on an unpatched Apache Tomcat server, followed by credential dumping and lateral movement through Active Directory, ultimately allowed access to an S3 bucket feeding an AI Co-Pilot agent.
  • 70% of organizations grant their AI systems more privileged access than a human in the same role, and those with over-privileged AI report a 76% incident rate versus only 17% for those enforcing least privilege.
  • Each new AI agent deployed connects to existing, already-exposed infrastructure, compounding the overall attack surface.

Why Legacy SIEMs Fail: The Move Toward a Security Data Lake | Mitiga — Optional

Mitiga built a purpose-built security data lake with a proprietary compute orchestration layer to deliver full-fidelity log retention and preemptive enrichment at a viable cost, enabling zero-impact breach prevention by giving SOCs immediate investigation-ready intelligence.

  • Standard data lakes fail security teams because they are optimized for analytics rather than forensics, leading many vendors to sample or drop logs to preserve their margins.
  • Mitiga built a proprietary compute orchestration layer on Airflow and Amazon EMR that reduces compute costs by over 50%, making full-fidelity log retention economically viable at enterprise scale.
  • The platform continuously transforms raw security logs into investigation-ready intelligence with forensic-grade enrichment, so security operations centers can immediately investigate and stop threats before material damage occurs.
  • The architecture enforces strict tenant-specific isolation and preserves complete audit trails, supporting compliance frameworks like HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOC 2 while keeping all data.
  • Full-fidelity, preemptively enriched data is positioned as a critical foundation for future autonomous, AI-driven security operations, as incomplete data would cause AI agents to hallucinate or miss threats.

5 Tips to Address the Cybersecurity Skills Gap — Optional

Organizations can immediately bridge the cybersecurity skills gap by using cloud managed detection and response (MDR) services, while building long-term resilience through upskilling, automation, and strategic hiring.

  • The cybersecurity skills gap now includes shortages in cloud security knowledge, incident response expertise, threat hunting skills, strategic thinking, and automation proficiency.
  • Upskilling existing team members through certifications, cross-training, and on-the-job learning can help close skills gaps.
  • Managed services like Cloud MDR provide immediate access to continuous threat monitoring, advanced incident response, and proactive threat hunting.
  • Automation and AI can amplify a team's effectiveness by handling repetitive tasks such as threat detection, alert triage, and workflow orchestration.
  • Retaining cybersecurity talent requires providing clear career growth paths, recognition, and work-life balance to combat burnout.

⚡ Weekly Recap: Browser Bugs, EDR Killers, TV Botnet, OpenBSD Flaw, Android Trojan, and More — Optional

This week's threats highlight a recurring theme: attackers don't need sophisticated zero-days when weak credentials, stale integrations, overly permissive browser extensions, and unpatched WordPress sites continue to provide easy, effective access.

  • The FortiBleed campaign targeted over 80,000 Fortinet FortiGate devices worldwide, using reused credentials and brute-force attacks, with suspected Russian-speaking threat actors.
  • The Gentlemen ransomware group developed GentleKiller, an EDR killer suite that disables over 400 processes from 48 security products before deploying encryptors.
  • The Rokarolla Android trojan targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps, uses 137 commands for complete device control, and hides its presence to exfiltrate data and intercept transactions.
  • A 27-year-old authentication bypass flaw in OpenBSD's PPP stack allowed attackers to completely sidestep Password Authentication Protocol by sending zero-length name and password fields.
  • The Popa Android TV box botnet, used for residential proxy traffic in ad fraud, was linked to publicly traded Israeli company Alarum Technologies, which embeds an SDK to turn consumer devices into internet relay nodes.

No ID, No Data For You — Optional

Securing AI agents demands both a workload identity and access layer—combining human and agent identities into a blended, ephemeral credential—and a data policy layer that inspects and governs agent traffic to prevent misuse, data leaks, and prompt attacks.

  • AI agents demand both an identity and access layer and a data policy layer because they lack built-in identity, behave unpredictably, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP) offers no native data control.
  • Aembit creates a blended identity that verifies the specific human user together with the specific agent workload before granting access, using just-in-time, ephemeral credentials injected at runtime.
  • MCP lacks a native data policy layer, meaning it cannot provide visibility, enforcement, or governance of data flows on its own.
  • Netskope's policy layer—via Agentic Broker, AI Gateway, and AI Guardrails—delivers real-time identification, risk scoring, data leak prevention, and defense against prompt injection and jailbreaking for agent traffic.
  • Integrating Aembit and Netskope eliminates the need to create or store static access tokens while logging every interaction and enforcing identity-based policies for defense in depth.

IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS vs CaaS: Cloud Models Compared — Optional

The article explains the differences between IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and CaaS cloud service models, emphasizing that choosing the right model depends on your team's technical expertise, security needs, and budget, with most enterprises combining multiple models for optimal cost and performance.

  • The cloud computing market is projected to reach between $905 billion and over $1 trillion in 2026.
  • 89% of enterprises use multiple cloud service models together as part of multi-cloud strategies.
  • The average organization now uses nearly 5 different cloud environments, spanning both public and private cloud.
  • 49% of cloud incidents involve API issues, highlighting a key risk in platform-as-a-service environments.
  • Most cloud security failures are projected to result from misconfigurations by 2026, making automated security tools essential.

item.title: PixelSmash RCE, FortiBleed Credential Harvesting, and ShapedPlugin Backdoor Highlight Supply Chain and AI Agent Threats
Compromised Argentine Microsoft 365 School Account Phishes U.S. K-12 District
Sapphire Sleet Mastra npm supply-chain attack and Prinz Eugen ransomware emerge
Sapphire Sleet npm Attack, Prinz Eugen Ransomware, and Gravity SMTP CVE-2026-4020 Exploitation
FortiBleed, SocGholish Disruption, AutoJack, and Texas TPWD Breach
item.description: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a concentrated set of developments involving internet-facing access infrastructure, malware delivery ecosystems, emerging AI-agent attack surfaces, and public-sector data exposure. The most operationally significant items are the reported compromise of tens of thousands of FortiGate devices in the FortiBleed campaign and the multinational disruption of SocGholish infrastructure, both tied to initial access at scale. CISA warned Fortinet customers about ongoing malicious activity targeting internet-accessible FortiGate appliances, with the FortiBleed campaign reported to have compromised 86,644 devices as of June 19, 2026. SOCRadar attributed the activity to Russian-speaking threat actors and reported that the campaign involved mass scanning for Fortinet remote login endpoints followed by password spraying using known username and password combinations. Generic administrative accounts and built-in Fortinet system accounts reportedly made up most compromised credentials, while organization-specific accounts accounted for 36.7%. In a separate but related initial-access development, Dutch law enforcement and counterparts from Canada, Germany, and the United States disrupted infrastructure associated with SocGholish, also known as FakeUpdates, under Operation Endgame. Authorities took down 106 servers and cleaned infections from 14,971 WordPress websites. SocGholish has operated since 2017 as a JavaScript-based downloader delivered through compromised websites impersonating browser or software updates. The SocGholish ecosystem has been significant because of its role as a conduit into downstream criminal operations. Reported beneficiaries or associated malware families include Evil Corp, LockBit, RansomHub, Dridex, Raspberry Robin, RomCom, Gholoader, MintsLoader, GhostWeaver, AsyncRAT, and NetSupport RAT. Shadowserver also noted use of domain shadowing, where attackers create malicious subdomains under legitimate domains to point to criminal-controlled infrastructure. Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain showing how a malicious web page loaded by an AI browsing agent could execute commands on the host running AutoGen Studio. The proof of concept used a Web Content Summarizer agent to launch calc.exe from the AutoGen Studio process, demonstrating host command execution through agent-driven web interaction. Microsoft reported no exploitation in the wild. The issue centered on a vulnerable MCP WebSocket handler present in AutoGen Studio PyPI pre-release builds 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, but not in the stable PyPI release 0.4.2.2. The exploit chain relied on a localhost trust check, MCP paths bypassing authentication middleware, and an endpoint that executed a command supplied directly in a request parameter. Maintainers hardened the GitHub main branch in commit b047730, though the article stated that the fix had not yet appeared in a PyPI release. The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach affecting 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers through its license system vendor. Exposed information may include driver’s license information, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and residential addresses. The agency stated that Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and financial information such as credit card data were not impacted. Texas Cyber Command discovered the intrusion and opened an investigation into the extent and impact of the unauthorized access. TPWD said it is working with the vendor to add new safeguards and enhanced monitoring services. The incident highlights continued exposure risk in third-party systems that handle government licensing and identity-related data at large scale.
